title: Refrigerator Energy Consumption Calculator | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Your Refrigerator's Annual Energy Consumption
description: Estimate your refrigerator's annual kWh consumption & electricity costs. Calculate based on power and duty cycle. Compare ENERGY STAR models. Free & instant.
seoKeywords: how much does a refrigerator cost to run
intro: This calculator estimates your refrigerator's **annual and monthly energy consumption in kWh**, plus its total electricity cost, based on three inputs: compressor wattage, duty cycle (the fraction of time the compressor actually runs), and your local electricity rate. Refrigerators are among the top three energy-consuming appliances in American homes, accounting for roughly 7–13% of total household electricity use (EIA, 2023). The core formula is: **kWh/year = Watts × 24 h × 365 days × Duty Cycle (decimal) ÷ 1,000**. A typical modern top-freezer refrigerator draws 100–200 W when its compressor is running and operates on a 30–50% duty cycle, yielding roughly 260–876 kWh/year. Understanding this figure helps you compare appliances before purchase, validate ENERGY STAR labels, and estimate payback periods when upgrading to a more efficient model.
useCases: Comparing two refrigerators before buying: a 120 W / 35% duty-cycle model (367 kWh/yr) vs. a 180 W / 45% unit (709 kWh/yr) reveals a $44/year operating cost difference at average U.S. rates.
Verifying an ENERGY STAR label: DOE test-procedure kWh figures can be cross-checked against your compressor nameplate wattage and measured duty cycle using a smart plug energy monitor.
Calculating payback period when replacing a 1990s refrigerator (~1,200 kWh/yr) with a 2024 ENERGY STAR model (~400 kWh/yr), saving ~800 kWh/yr (~$104/yr at $0.13/kWh).
Sizing an off-grid solar or battery backup system: knowing your fridge draws 525 kWh/yr (~1.44 kWh/day) lets you spec the correct battery bank and solar panel array capacity.
Checking whether a second garage refrigerator justifies its cost: a 20-year-old secondary fridge often consumes 800–1,200 kWh/yr, adding $104–$156/yr to your electricity bill.
Estimating carbon footprint: 525.6 kWh/yr × 0.386 kg CO₂/kWh (U.S. average grid, EPA 2023) = ~203 kg CO₂ per year for one appliance.

fields.label: Operating Duty Cycle (%)
fields.help: Percentage of time the compressor runs.

explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The refrigerator compressor does not run continuously — it cycles on and off to maintain the set temperature. The **duty cycle** expresses what fraction of each hour the compressor is actually energized.

```
Annual kWh = (P_watts × 24 × 365 × DC) / 1000
           = (P_watts × 8,760 × DC) / 1000

Monthly kWh = Annual kWh / 12

Annual Cost ($) = Annual kWh × Rate ($/kWh)
```

Where:
- `P_watts` = compressor running wattage (from nameplate or smart plug measurement)
- `DC` = duty cycle as a decimal (e.g., 40% → 0.40)
- `8,760` = hours in a year (24 × 365)
- `Rate` = your utility's electricity rate in $/kWh (U.S. average: $0.1611/kWh as of April 2024, BLS)

---

## Reference Table

| Refrigerator Type | Typical Power (W) | Typical Duty Cycle | Est. kWh/yr | ENERGY STAR Threshold (kWh/yr)* |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Compact / Mini (< 5 cu ft) | 50–80 W | 25–35% | 110–245 | ≤ 215 |
| Top-Freezer (14–20 cu ft) | 100–150 W | 30–45% | 263–591 | ≤ 423 |
| Side-by-Side (22–26 cu ft) | 150–200 W | 40–55% | 526–962 | ≤ 585 |
| French Door (25–30 cu ft) | 120–180 W | 35–50% | 368–789 | ≤ 569 |
| Bottom-Freezer (18–22 cu ft) | 110–160 W | 30–45% | 289–630 | ≤ 477 |
| Vintage (pre-1993, 18 cu ft) | 250–400 W | 50–65% | 1,095–2,277 | N/A |

*ENERGY STAR 2024 thresholds vary by volume; values shown are representative mid-size examples (EPA ENERGY STAR program).

**Average U.S. electricity rate used for cost estimates: $0.1611/kWh (BLS, April 2024).**

---

## Typical Case Examples

### Example 1 — Modern Top-Freezer (from calculator example)
- Compressor power: **150 W**, Duty cycle: **40%**, Rate: **$0.13/kWh**
- Annual kWh: 150 × 8,760 × 0.40 / 1,000 = **525.6 kWh/yr**
- Monthly kWh: 525.6 / 12 = **43.8 kWh/month**
- Annual cost: 525.6 × $0.13 = **$68.33/yr**

### Example 2 — Large Side-by-Side (older model)
- Compressor power: **190 W**, Duty cycle: **50%**, Rate: **$0.1611/kWh**
- Annual kWh: 190 × 8,760 × 0.50 / 1,000 = **832.2 kWh/yr**
- Monthly kWh: **69.4 kWh/month**
- Annual cost: 832.2 × $0.1611 = **$134.07/yr**

### Example 3 — Off-Grid Mini Fridge
- Compressor power: **60 W**, Duty cycle: **30%**, Rate: **$0.25/kWh** (remote/solar rate)
- Annual kWh: 60 × 8,760 × 0.30 / 1,000 = **157.7 kWh/yr**
- Monthly kWh: **13.1 kWh/month**
- Annual cost: 157.7 × $0.25 = **$39.42/yr**

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Using the total appliance wattage instead of compressor wattage.** The nameplate or door sticker may list a maximum draw (including defrost heaters, fans, and lighting). The compressor-only wattage is typically 60–80% of that figure. Using the inflated number can overestimate consumption by 25–40%.

2. **Setting the duty cycle to 100%.** Compressors cycle; they never run 100% of the time under normal conditions (unless the appliance is faulty or the door seal is broken). A 100% duty cycle assumption will roughly double or triple the true kWh estimate.

3. **Ignoring defrost cycles.** Frost-free refrigerators run an electric defrost heater for ~20–30 minutes every 6–12 hours (~150–600 W). This adds roughly 30–80 kWh/year that the compressor-only formula does not capture. For full accuracy, add an estimated defrost energy term or use a kill-a-watt meter for 48 hours.

4. **Using a national average rate when local rates differ dramatically.** Hawaii averages $0.39/kWh while Louisiana averages $0.099/kWh (EIA, 2024). Using $0.13 in Hawaii underestimates annual cost by ~67%.

5. **Confusing kVA or kW on the nameplate with running watts.** Some labels show locked-rotor amps (LRA) for the starting surge, not the steady running current. Use the "RLA" (Running Load Amps) value multiplied by voltage for accurate running watts.

6. **Not accounting for ambient temperature.** A refrigerator in a 90 °F garage runs a significantly higher duty cycle (55–70%) than the same unit in a 70 °F kitchen (30–45%), substantially increasing annual consumption.

---

## Related Calculators

Since no related slugs are available for this calculator at this time, check back soon for links to our **Air Conditioner Energy Cost Calculator**, **Washing Machine kWh Calculator**, and **Solar Panel Payback Period Calculator** — all coming to Hacé Cuentas.

faq.q: What is a normal duty cycle for a household refrigerator?
faq.a: Most frost-free refrigerators in a 70 °F kitchen run their compressors between 25% and 50% of the time. The DOE standard test procedure uses a 70 °F ambient at 37 °F/0 °F (fresh food/freezer) set points, which typically yields a 30–45% duty cycle. Older units or those in warm garages can exceed 60%.

faq.q: How do I measure my refrigerator's actual wattage and duty cycle?
faq.a: Plug the refrigerator into a kill-a-watt meter (e.g., P3 P4400) or a smart plug with energy monitoring (e.g., Emporia Vue, Kasa EP25). Monitor for at least 24–48 hours to capture multiple compressor and defrost cycles. The device will display both total kWh and average wattage, from which you can back-calculate the effective duty cycle as: DC = (kWh ÷ hours) × 1000 ÷ compressor_watts.

faq.q: What kWh/year does an ENERGY STAR-certified refrigerator use?
faq.a: ENERGY STAR 2024 requires refrigerators to be at least 15% more efficient than the federal DOE minimum standard. For a typical 20 cu ft top-freezer model, the ENERGY STAR threshold is roughly 400–430 kWh/yr. The most efficient models (ENERGY STAR Most Efficient) can reach 300–350 kWh/yr. These figures are measured under the DOE 10 CFR Part 430 test procedure.

faq.q: How much electricity does the average U.S. refrigerator use per year?
faq.a: According to the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) 2020 Residential Energy Consumption Survey (RECS), the average U.S. household refrigerator consumes approximately 448 kWh/year. Households with a second refrigerator add roughly another 400–600 kWh/yr. Older pre-1990 units can exceed 1,200 kWh/yr.

faq.q: What is the average residential electricity rate in the United States?
faq.a: As of early 2024, the U.S. average retail residential electricity price is approximately $0.1611/kWh according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) CPI data and EIA Electric Power Monthly. Rates range from about $0.099/kWh in Louisiana to $0.39/kWh in Hawaii. Always use your own utility bill rate for accurate cost estimates.

faq.q: Why does my calculated kWh differ from the kWh label on my refrigerator's yellow EnergyGuide sticker?
faq.a: The EnergyGuide label uses the DOE-standardized test procedure (10 CFR Part 430), conducted at a fixed 70 °F ambient and specific door-opening patterns. Your real-world usage may differ due to ambient temperature, how often you open the door, how full the fridge is, and whether the door seals are intact. Real consumption is typically within ±15% of the label value under normal home conditions.

faq.q: Does a full refrigerator use more or less energy than an empty one?
faq.a: A moderately full refrigerator is generally more efficient. Food mass acts as thermal ballast, absorbing heat and reducing the number of compressor cycles needed after a door opening. However, an over-packed fridge restricts airflow, forcing the compressor to run longer. The sweet spot is roughly 75–85% full. The USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service recommends keeping the fridge at 40 °F or below regardless of load.

faq.q: How much CO₂ does my refrigerator emit annually?
faq.a: Using the EPA's 2023 U.S. average grid emission factor of 0.386 kg CO₂e per kWh, a refrigerator consuming 525 kWh/yr emits roughly 202 kg CO₂e per year (~446 lbs). Switching to a 350 kWh/yr ENERGY STAR model would cut that to ~135 kg CO₂e/yr, saving ~67 kg CO₂e annually. EPA's eGRID tool provides state-level emission factors for more precise regional estimates.

faq.q: Is it worth replacing a 15-year-old refrigerator with a new ENERGY STAR model?
faq.a: A 2009-era full-size refrigerator typically consumes 600–900 kWh/yr, while a 2024 ENERGY STAR equivalent uses ~380–430 kWh/yr — a saving of 200–500 kWh/yr. At $0.16/kWh, that's $32–$80/yr in savings. A new ENERGY STAR unit costs roughly $700–$1,200, implying a simple payback period of 9–37 years on energy alone. Utility rebates (often $25–$150) and the avoided repair costs of an aging compressor often improve that calculation significantly.

sources.name: U.S. EIA – Residential Energy Consumption Survey (RECS 2020)
EPA ENERGY STAR – Refrigerators Key Product Criteria
DOE – 10 CFR Part 430 Appliance Standards (Refrigerators & Freezers)
BLS – Average Energy Prices, U.S. City Average (CPI)

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
